one_line_takeaway: LanG is an integrated, governance-first open-source agentic SOC platform whose own benchmarks show a 96.2% Snort/Suricata rule-deployment acceptance rate for its best finetuned model, an 87.5% kill-chain reconstruction accuracy, and a 98.1% F1 two-layer prompt-injection guardrail with zero measured false positives, but its core-detector macro-F1 (41.3%) and self-scored platform comparison temper the headline numbers.
key_finding: Rule generator: on Snort2/Snort3/Suricata combined (the platform's primary IDS targets), deployment acceptance rates against live engines were 84.7% (Phi-3-mini), 90.8% (Qwen3-1.7-base), 96.2% (CodeLlama-7B), and 95.5% (Mistral-7B); YARA acceptance was much lower at 38.5-78.5%; overall (all 4 formats, 800 rules) acceptance ranged 73.1-90.2%, with Mistral-7B highest overall (90.2%). Syntactic validity (held-out n=5,357) ranged from 88% to 93.5% average (Qwen3-1.7-base highest), with post-processing adding 3.2-5.1 percentage points. Correlation engine: average incident-grouping F1 = 87.0% (precision 89.0%, recall 86.0%) across the 6 scenarios, best on WebProtocols (F1=91.0%), worst on RemoteSystemDiscovery (F1=83.0%). Attack reconstructor: average kill-chain chain-order accuracy = 87.5% and average Bayesian posterior score = 0.78. Detection models: LanG-NetSentinel (binary) reached accuracy=99.0%, weighted F1=99.0%; LanG-ThreatGuard (10-class) reached accuracy=91.0%, weighted F1=91.0% but macro F1 only 41.3% due to severe class imbalance (near-perfect on dominant classes like DoS/Normal, much weaker on rare classes like Shellcode/Worms). Automated detection latency (Tauto, phases T1-T4) was ~21-22ms per alert; machine-side MTTD including LLM enrichment (Tmachine = Tauto+T5) was ~1.58s. Guardrail: Layer-1-only regex achieved 100% precision, aggregate F1=94.8%, with prompt-injection recall of 76.7%; adding the Llama Prompt Guard 2 semantic layer raised prompt-injection recall to 93.3% and aggregate F1 to 98.1%, with zero block-level false positives across all 70 benign samples and mean Layer-2 latency of ~39ms (invoked on only ~20% of inputs that pass Layer 1 clean). Six false negatives (4 prompt injection, 2 encoded payload) remained undetected by both layers.
core_contribution: LanG is an open-source, governance-aware agentic AI platform for unified SOC operations built from a Governance-MCP-Agentic AI-Security layered architecture. Its own five claimed contributions are: (C1) a Unified Incident Context Record (UICR) schema plus a correlation engine that groups related events via shared keys/IoC fingerprints/IP+time-window overlap and computes a 0-100 triage score; (C2) a five-node LangGraph agentic SOC pipeline (Ingest/Detect -> Classify -> Human Review 1 -> Analyze Logs -> Propose Rules -> Human Review 2 -> Deploy) with two mandatory human-in-the-loop checkpoints; (C3) an LLM-based detection rule generator that QLoRA-finetunes four open base models (Phi-3-mini-3.8B, CodeLlama-7B, Mistral-7B, Qwen3-1.7-base) on a curated multi-source dataset to emit Snort 2/3, Suricata, and YARA rules; (C4) a three-phase Attack Scenario Reconstructor combining an 8-hook weighted correlation graph, Louvain community detection, LLM-driven hypothesis generation, and Bayesian posterior scoring to produce kill-chain-coloured attack graphs with STIX/PDF export; and (C5) a Governance-MCP-Agentic AI-Security layered architecture in which all agent tools are exposed via MCP and mediated by an AI Governance Policy Engine (seven sub-policies: access restriction, security policy, responsible-AI, data privacy, IP protection, inter-agent governance, model protection) plus a two-layer guardrail pipeline (regex + a finetuned Llama Prompt Guard 2-86M semantic classifier). The platform also ships two production threat-detection adapters (LanG-NetSentinel, LanG-ThreatGuard) built on a repurposed Llama-Prompt-Guard-2-86M backbone, and integrates a third detector (LanG-SecLlama) from the authors' own prior work [63]. The system is designed for MSSP multi-tenant deployment, fully local inference via Ollama, and is compared feature-by-feature against eight existing SOC/SOAR/SIEM platforms.
threat_model: The paper's own threat model is defensive/operational, not an attack-research threat model: it treats the LLM agents themselves as an attack surface that must be governed. Threats explicitly guarded against within the platform are (i) prompt injection and jailbreak attempts against the LLM agents (input sanitizer with 18/19 compiled regex block+warn patterns, plus the Llama Prompt Guard 2 semantic classifier), (ii) output-side risks such as destructive commands (rm -rf, DROP TABLE), credential/API-key leakage, and data-exfiltration URLs (output validator with 9 command + 6 credential + 1 exfiltration regex patterns), (iii) unauthorized/excessive tool access and privilege escalation by agents (RBAC with deny-by-default access control, max delegation depth 5, scope inheritance for inter-agent delegation), (iv) cross-tenant/lateral-movement risk in MSSP multi-client deployments (per-tenant DB isolation, trust-boundary enforcement, failure isolation), and (v) resource exhaustion via a sliding-window rate limiter (default 30 calls/60s). The paper does not present novel offensive attacks against LLMs; it cites prior prompt-injection/jailbreak literature (HOUYI, OWASP LLM Top 10) as the threats it defends against rather than contributing new attack techniques.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Section VII.A): L1 stub/hard-coded authentication unsuitable for production, requiring future integration with enterprise IdPs (OAuth2/SAML/LDAP); L2 local LLMs (1.7B-8B parameters) have a lower reasoning-fidelity ceiling than frontier models for complex multi-step hypothesis generation, with some hidden edge cases as a residual failure risk; L3 the Phase-2 correlation algorithm is O(N^2) in the number of events, mitigated only by a top-N cutoff (default 100) that may exclude relevant low-ranked events; L4 SQLite-based persistence does not support concurrent multi-process writes, limiting high-throughput MSSP deployments without migrating to PostgreSQL/TimescaleDB. Beyond the authors' own list: the macro-F1 of LanG-ThreatGuard (41.3%) reveals the 10-class detector performs poorly on rare attack classes despite a strong weighted F1/accuracy headline; the LanG-SecLlama detector's numbers are simply imported from the authors' prior paper [63] rather than re-evaluated here; YARA rule generation deployability (38.5-78.5%) is substantially weaker than Snort/Suricata; the eight-platform comparison table is a self-defined, self-scored qualitative feature matrix rather than a blinded or third-party evaluation, and LanG is scored as fully supporting all ten features it defines, which is a structurally favorable framing; and the guardrail benchmark, attack-reconstruction corpus, and validation of 50 reconstructed scenarios were all constructed/labeled by the paper's own developers rather than independent annotators.

datasets_used: Curated multi-source rule-generation corpus (Emerging Threats Suricata rulesets v5.0/6.0.20/7.0.3, abuse.ch SSL blacklist and URLhaus IDS rules, PT Research AttackDetection, Snort2/Snort3 derived via syntax transformation, Yara-Rules/Neo23x0/ReversingLabs/Elastic protections-artifacts/BartBlaze, synthetic MITRE ATT&CK technique descriptions and zero-day templates): 42,856 train / 5,357 val / 5,357 test
BCCC-CIRA-CIC-DoHBrw-2020 (binary DNS-over-HTTPS traffic, 1,000 test samples) for LanG-NetSentinel
CIC-UNBW24 (10-class, derived from UNSW-NB15 family, 4,999 test samples, SMOTE-balanced) for LanG-ThreatGuard
UNSW-NB15 (from authors' prior work [63]) for LanG-SecLlama

Six MITRE ATT&CK-annotated SOC log corpora generated with an open-source log generator: soc-compliance-audit (273K entries), soc-incident-response (159K), soc-malware-analysis (163K), soc-network-defense (248K), soc-simulation (153K), soc-threat-hunting (215K)
Attack-reconstruction corpus: 12,400 events across 6 scenarios (BruteForce, Exfiltration, WebProtocols, RemoteSystemDiscovery, AccountManipulation, LocalDataSearch)
Self-constructed guardrail benchmark: 232 samples (162 adversarial across 7 categories, 70 benign across 5 categories)
quantitative_results: Overall (4-format, 800 rules) acceptance: 73.1% / 87.8% / 89.9% / 90.2% for Phi-3-mini / Qwen3-1.7-base / CodeLlama-7B / Mistral-7B respectively
Average syntactic validity rate across formats: 93.5% (Qwen3-1.7-base, highest); all models >88%
Post-processing validity gain: +3.2 to +5.1 percentage points
Correlation engine average F1 = 87.0% (precision 89.0%, recall 86.0%); range F1 83.0% (RemoteSystemDiscovery) to 91.0% (WebProtocols)
Attack reconstructor average kill-chain accuracy = 87.5%, average Bayesian score = 0.78
LanG-NetSentinel: accuracy 99.0%, weighted F1 99.0%, mean confidence 99.8%
LanG-ThreatGuard: accuracy 91.0%, weighted F1 91.0%, macro F1 41.3%; benign F1 98.0%, exploits F1 70.0%
LanG-SecLlama (from prior work [63], reused not re-evaluated): accuracy 95%, weighted F1 95%
Automated detection latency Tauto ≈ 21-22ms; machine-side MTTD Tmachine ≈ 1.58s (T5 LLM enrichment mean 1.56s, median 1.19s, P95 2.02s)

Guardrail: Layer-1-only F1 = 94.8%, precision 100%, prompt-injection recall 76.7%; Layer1+2 F1 = 98.1%, prompt-injection recall 93.3%, aggregate recall 96.3%, zero block-level false positives across 70 benign samples, Layer-2 mean latency ≈39ms on ~20% of inputs
End-to-end per-alert pipeline latency range: ≈4.0s (Llama3.2 + FT Phi-3-mini) to ≈16.0s (Llama3.1 + FT Mistral-7B)
Feature comparison: LanG scores 10/10 full checkmarks vs. 1-3/10 for the eight compared platforms (Splunk SOAR 3, XSOAR 2, MS Sentinel 2, Elastic Security 1, TheHive 1, Wazuh 2, Shuffle 2, MISP 2)
baselines_compared: Four finetuned base models compared against each other for rule generation: Phi-3-mini-3.8B, CodeLlama-7B, Mistral-7B, Qwen3-1.7-base (no external/non-finetuned LLM baseline reported)
Ablation baseline: rule validity with vs. without RulePostProcessor
Guardrail ablation baseline: Layer-1-only (regex) vs. Layer-1+2 (regex + semantic classifier)
Qualitative feature-matrix comparison (not a shared quantitative benchmark) against 8 platforms: Splunk SOAR, Cortex XSOAR, Microsoft Sentinel, Elastic Security, TheHive, Wazuh, Shuffle, MISP
